item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the imagery and the idea here so I'm upvoting, but I do think that the message doesn't come across as clearly as intended without the interview. If it had been placed after the forum post rather than before, I think it might have gotten a better reaction, rather than deleting it altogether. I also don't think there's any reason to leave it unclassified- it's kind of a tough call between Euclid and Keter, but I agree with the above poster that they wouldn't leave it unclassfied for so long.

But still, I like it overall, so a +1 with some reservations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting that the sixth edition cover doesn't seem to have any masks on it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this overall. The image of faceless children is sufficiently creepy for me to give this a +1.

That said, I have a few knitpicks

  • I don't think the Foundation would have a scip left in pending class for nearly 50 years. I'd want to see some reasoning if you were going to keep it.
  • I'm a bit confused on how the missing child from 1971 is incorporated into the story. Are all the missing children incorporated in some way or was the 1971 case a one time thing. You could add summaries for a few of the editions linking the plot of the book to details relating to the missing kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe so, but in the end I didn't even have theses questions/answers in mind when I came to the end of the scip. I went back and read the interview in the history and, while I understand why you cut it out, I feel like it provides a certain piece of direction the writing would otherwise not have. I'd recommend adding something else to replace it. Less direct than what you wrote, but something that still conveys this feeling.

When I first ended the skip without reading the interview I asked myself, why were theses children being taken away? Why is edition #6 undocumented? (#4 I rationalized was created in the tiny amount of time right after Child #3 was faceremoved but right before Child #4 was faceremoved). Where are the children now?

+1 but with some reticence

item.title: Update
item.content:encoded:
Due to overwhelming public consensus, I've gone ahead and removed the interview section of this article. Personally, I thought it was too ambiguous, but I guess I kinda forced my interpretation of the article in the dialogue which, ironically, is against what I was trying to do with this whole thing. Sometimes you have to kill your darlings, I guess.

[+] bigger reasons with spoilers and authorial intent
[–] cool

The interview was, I suppose, me trying to nudge the reader towards the symbolic side of things, where Masquerade acts kind of ideological "self-help" mechanisms that are simultaneously self-destructive but also, in the short-term, helpful. I had solipsism in mind when writing this, but I suppose the metaphor works for these in general. The way I did this was dry and without subtext, which understandably made the interview feel overly-expository — that said, I also feel that without the interview, this may be too undetectable.

At the same time, I felt I also had to nudge the reader towards the physical side of things, which made it a bit of an incoherent, confused mess. Kids *are* going missing, and they are being used to fuel new editions — but that's not what Clyde was talking about 3 sentences ago.

The main mechanical purpose of the interview, though, was to make the reader question why Masquerade is collecting masks so her actions are scrutinised a little bit more and not just disregarded as "spooky storytelling".

Anyway, that's why I thought the interview was a good idea and, in hindsight, probably wasn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting but I agree with the commenters above that the interview did feel too in your face. The Parawatch post already does a good job of showing what the book was about, and I feel like having the author be voiceless adds to the mystery. It feel jarring compared to the containment procedures and the description which do a good job of keeping on that sweet spot between vague and explicit. The table especially does a great job at this. The super minimalist nature of it really sets the mood. I also love the cover image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, while I think this is deserving of an upvote… I also agree the podcast interview doesn’t serve as well as the book’s summary in the last addendum.

I’d either cut the interview or make it more obfuscated, mysterious

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is dope, but I'm hesitant on the upvote. The interview is too expository for my tastes and is invalidated by the final addendum which, all things considered, better conveys the core themes here. Otherwise this strikes a fairly excellent balance between obfuscation and elucidation.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
．．．

— OFF OST #5: Not Safe

  • This was JackalRelated's draft for SCPDraftswap, edited by your truly. Hopefully it works out.
  • Image sources: 1* and 2*

Invaluable Critters:

  • Alces_alces — SCP-4902: Keep Your Cool
  • RockTeethMothEyes — SCP-4814: Woodpecker
  • Uncle Nicolini — SCP-4026: Guardian "angel"
  • cybersqyd — SCP-4518: MBMBMBMBMBMBMBaM
  • Lt Flops — Dr Michaels is not in danger

AAAAAA
